feedback:
The model demonstrates solid overall capability in Defense Evasion identification tasks, achieving an exact match accuracy of 62.5% (10/16 questions) with a strong average Jaccard of 0.857. The model is well-calibrated in terms of answer count, averaging 3.44 predicted answers versus 3.94 gold answers, indicating a mild but consistent under-selection tendency rather than over-selection. Parse quality is perfect with 0% parse errors.

Key strengths: The model correctly identifies classic Defense Evasion indicators including software packing (UPX, MPRESS, PECompact), high-entropy sections, process injection via WriteProcessMemory, reflective code loading, timestomping, file deletion, and masquerading. It performs perfectly on Hard difficulty questions in several cases and shows strong performance on ransomware and remcos attack families.

Primary weakness is systematic under-selection: in 6 of the 6 incorrect answers, the model missed one or more correct options while not adding false positives. The model never over-selects. Specifically, the model struggles to recognize: (1) firewall modification via netsh.exe (T1562.004) as Defense Evasion - missed in questions 0 and 11; (2) file deletion/indicator removal (T1070.004) as Defense Evasion when performed by a child process - missed in question 1; (3) masquerading via dropping to Windows system directory (T1036) - missed in question 1; (4) process injection (T1055) as Defense Evasion in the context of Worm.Ludbaruma - incorrectly included in question 5 (over-selected in that case); (5) Disable or Modify Tools (T1562.001) via registry-based task manager disabling - missed in question 12; (6) proxy settings modification (T1112) and file marks for deletion as Defense Evasion in the WannaCry-style report - completely wrong in question 7 (worst performance); (7) file marks for deletion and proxy modification as Defense Evasion in question 9.

The most problematic question was question 7 (ransomware/WannaCry-style, Hard), where the model selected {A, B, C, D} but the gold was {A, C, F}, indicating confusion about what constitutes Defense Evasion versus Discovery when analyzing service-access patterns and internet cache queries.

For training, the model should be exposed to: (1) more questions distinguishing T1562.004 (firewall modification) as Defense Evasion; (2) questions on indicator removal behaviors including proxy deletion and file deletion; (3) ransomware Defense Evasion patterns where child processes perform evasive actions; (4) distinguishing Discovery from Defense Evasion for ambiguous API calls like OpenSCManager, OpenService, registry cache queries; (5) T1562.001 via registry-based security tool disabling. The model's under-selection pattern suggests it is conservative and needs to be trained to recognize a broader set of Defense Evasion sub-techniques.
strengths: Perfect parse rate: all 16 model answers are well-formed and contain valid option letter sets.
Strong exact match accuracy of 62.5% on Defense Evasion identification tasks.
High average Jaccard of 0.857 indicating strong partial credit performance even on missed questions.
Excellent performance on Hard difficulty questions: 9/13 exact matches.
Correct identification of software packing indicators (UPX, MPRESS, PECompact, high-entropy sections) as T1027.002/T1027.009.
Reliable identification of process injection (WriteProcessMemory, RtlCreateUserThread) as Defense Evasion.
Correct recognition of timestomping (T1070.006) via FileTimeToLocalFileTime and GetSystemTimeAsFileTime.
Strong performance on ransomware family questions: 3/4 exact matches.
Strong performance on remcos family questions: 2/2 exact matches.
Never over-selects: all errors are under-selection, indicating conservative but grounded reasoning.

weaknesses.area_name: under-selection of firewall modification as Defense Evasion
weaknesses.description: The model consistently fails to recognize netsh.exe firewall rule modification (T1562.004) as a Defense Evasion technique. This was missed in question 0 (MSIL.Bladabindi spawning netsh.exe with 'add allowedprogram') and question 11 (netsh.exe advfirewall add rule for svchost.exe). The model likely does not associate firewall modification with Defense Evasion tactic.

The model misses file deletion (T1070.004) and proxy settings deletion (T1112) as Defense Evasion in multiple questions. In question 1 it missed E (file deletion by microsofthelp.exe) and H (masquerading via system directory drop). In question 9 it missed C (file deletion) and E (proxy modification). These are clearly documented in the detonation reports.

Question 7 (CVE-2017-0147/WannaCry-style) was the worst-performing question. The model selected {A, B, C, D} but gold was {A, C, F}. The model incorrectly included B (OpenSCManager/OpenService access) and D (reading cryptographic GUID) as Defense Evasion, while missing F (proxy settings modification via SETVAL). This indicates confusion between Discovery/lateral movement behaviors and Defense Evasion in the context of worm-style ransomware.

weaknesses.area_name: under-selection of Disable or Modify Tools (T1562.001) via registry
weaknesses.description: In question 12, the model missed H (svchost.exe disabling Windows Task Manager via HKCU registry modification, T1562.001). This is a direct registry-based security tool disabling behavior that is clearly documented in the Ransom.HydraCrypt report but was not recognized.

weaknesses.area_name: semantic confusion between Discovery and Defense Evasion for service/registry access APIs
weaknesses.description: The model incorrectly included OpenSCManager and OpenService calls as Defense Evasion in question 7, and reading cryptographic GUID as Defense Evasion. These are Discovery behaviors. Conversely, it sometimes misses actual Defense Evasion behaviors. The model needs better calibration on the tactic boundary.

weaknesses.area_name: under-selection of masquerading via system directory drops
weaknesses.description: In question 1, the model missed H (hash.exe dropping microsofthelp.exe into Windows system directory as masquerading T1036). Dropping executables to system directories to masquerade as legitimate binaries is a clear Defense Evasion indicator but was not selected.

weaknesses.area_name: over-selection in Worm.Ludbaruma process injection classification
weaknesses.description: In question 5 (Worm.Ludbaruma), the model selected E (VirtualAllocEx/WriteProcessMemory as Process Injection T1055) but the gold answer excluded E and included F (file deletion) and I (file extension change). The model incorrectly classified process injection as a Defense Evasion technique in this specific context where the gold answer did not include it.
The model achieved only 1/3 exact matches on medium difficulty questions (33%), compared to 9/13 on hard (69%). Medium questions 0 and 13 were both missed due to under-selection.
